abstract: Many Christian families today struggle with a lack of fulfillment due to a misunderstanding of Biblical family management. This research explores the theological underpinnings of Psalm 127:1-5 and its practical implementation within the context of contemporary family life. Utilizing a qualitative descriptive method and a literature-based approach, the study examines the essential roles of the household as a place of residence, security, and provision, while identifying children as a divine inheritance and a source of parental joy. The analysis highlights that human effort—whether in building a home, securing a city, or pursuing a livelihood—is ultimately futile ("vain") without the active involvement and authority of God. The findings conclude that a happy and successful family is not the product of mere intellectual or financial capacity, but of a life centered on God’s will. By recognizing God as the ultimate architect of the home, families can experience genuine harmony and avoid the metaphorical "vanity" of self-reliance, ensuring that their household remains firm even amidst the challenges of the modern era.
